funders.blurb: NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) — successor to the U.S. AI Safety Institute — conducts voluntary evaluations of AI capabilities for national security risks and develops security guidelines and standards for AI on behalf of the U.S. government.

apply.notes: The NIST AI Consortium (the membership body through which CAISI conducts collaborative research) accepts letters of interest from all organizations that can contribute expertise, products, data, or models — open on an ongoing basis with biannual review periods; first review began June 2026. Participants enter a CRADA with NIST. No funding is provided — this is a collaborative research arrangement, not a grant program. CAISI does not operate a public grant program.

funders.name: Alfred P. Sloan Foundation
funders.blurb: Funds AI-in-science transparency and open-source research software via $100-400K rolling grants. Metascience & AI Postdoc Fellowships target reproducibility. Relevant to academic open-source AI and human-AI interaction.
funders.thesis: "To continually monitor the landscape of emerging technology with the aim of identifying emerging new sectors, tools, and methods of working that we believe have potential, but which may not yet receive funding from mainstream/other sources." The Open Source in Science program develops tools, norms, and institutions for open-source research software, hardware, and models.

apply.notes: Submit a two-page letter of inquiry to program email; no fixed deadlines on any program page. Separate LOI channel for Open Source in Science via same staff.

funders.name: National AI Research Resource Pilot
funders.blurb: NSF-led infrastructure giving U.S. researchers GPU compute, cloud credits, datasets, and models at no cost. Startup track: up to 2,000 GPU-hrs/3mo with 3-week turnaround; full allocations reviewed monthly.
funders.thesis: The NAIRR Pilot's mission is to democratize AI research by "connecting U.S. researchers and educators to AI compute, data, software, models, training, and educational materials," reducing infrastructure barriers that concentrate AI R&D at large institutions.

apply.notes: Full allocations: submit by the 15th of each month; decisions by end of the following month. Apply at submit-nairr.xras.org with an ACCESS ID. Startup track (~2,000 GPU-hrs/3mo): 3-week turnaround. U.S.-based institutions only.

funders.blurb: A philanthropic investment firm created by eBay founder Pierre Omidyar and Pam Omidyar, now focused on the culture, governance, and business of technology; in 2023 it launched a dedicated effort backing the inclusive and responsible development of generative AI with an initial $30 million commitment.
funders.thesis: Omidyar Network's guiding question is "How do we hardwire humanity into the governance of tech?" It works to ensure technology policy serves the public interest, warning that without "guardrails to set the rules of the road" AI will carry forward "extractive, invasive, and often predatory data practices."

apply.notes: Does not accept unsolicited proposals or funding requests ("we do not have the capacity to manage and vet the hundreds of unsolicited requests that come in each week"); sources partners through research, referrals from existing partners and other funders, events, and media. A separate Tech Journalism Fund runs open calls for US-based journalists ($5k-$25k).

grantees.blurb: MIRI pioneered technical AI alignment research (decision theory, agent foundations) and has since pivoted to policy advocacy focused on halting superintelligence development through internationally coordinated moratoriums.

CSET is a Georgetown University policy think tank that produces data-driven analysis on the national-security implications of AI — covering compute/semiconductor supply chains, US-China AI competition, and AI applications in defense — to inform policymakers on AI governance.

80,000 Hours is a nonprofit that builds the AI safety field by providing career research, one-on-one advising, and a job board to direct talented people into high-impact AI safety roles across technical research, policy, and operations.

RAND Corporation is a global nonprofit policy research organization whose AI-safety-relevant work (sized here by its AI governance and safety grants received) spans compute governance, catastrophic-risk policy, AI safety institute design, and tabletop exercises on AI-enabled threats.

UC Berkeley hosts CHAI (Center for Human-Compatible AI, founded by Stuart Russell) and BAIR, conducting foundational AI safety research in value alignment, interpretability, and robustness (sized here by its AI-safety-relevant grants received).

MIT (sized here by its AI-safety-relevant grants received) hosts the CSAIL Algorithmic Alignment Group on technical alignment, the MIT AI Risk Initiative maintaining a 1,700+ risk taxonomy used by the UN and EU AI Office, and the AI Agent Index evaluating safety transparency in deployed agents.

BERI provides operational, financial, and administrative support (HR, grant management, compliance) to university AI-safety and x-risk research groups such as CHAI, FHI, and SERI, enabling them to focus on research rather than bureaucracy.

Lightcone Infrastructure builds and maintains LessWrong and the AI Alignment Forum — the primary online platforms for AI safety and rationalist research discussion — alongside the Lighthaven event venue in Berkeley, providing core community infrastructure for the field.

Epoch AI is a research nonprofit that tracks AI training compute, model capabilities, and hardware investment trends to forecast transformative AI timelines and inform AI safety strategy for grantmakers and policymakers.

BlueDot Impact is a non-profit talent accelerator that runs AI safety courses and career-transition programs to build the pipeline of professionals working on beneficial AI, having trained 7,000+ people at labs, government agencies, and AI safety organizations since 2022.

Stanford University conducts AI safety research through the Stanford Center for AI Safety and Stanford HAI, spanning technical alignment, trustworthy AI systems, and policy/governance — sized here by its AI-safety-relevant grants received.

GovAI is an independent nonprofit that conducts rigorous research on AI policy, threat modeling, and institutional risk management to help governments and decision-makers navigate the transition to advanced AI.

OpenMined is a nonprofit building open-source privacy-preserving AI infrastructure (PySyft/Syft) for federated computation across private datasets without centralizing data, with AI-safety-relevant work on privacy-preserving model evaluations (NIST partnership) and AI governance policy (UK Frontier AI Taskforce, U.S. Senate AI roadmap).

CNAS is a nonpartisan national security think tank (sized here by its AI-safety-relevant policy work) that researches AI governance frameworks, international stability risks from advanced AI, and federal model assessment standards for the DoD and intelligence community.

AI Safety Support provided field-building services — coaching, community networking, and career resources — to early-career and independent researchers entering the AI safety ecosystem, before pausing operations indefinitely in July 2023 due to funding exhaustion and founder burnout.

ESPR is a free 10-day residential camp in the UK for mathematically talented 16–19 year olds that teaches rationality, AI safety, and AI evaluation to build the pipeline of future alignment-aware researchers.
